skill.name: emil-design-eng
skill.description: This skill encodes Emil Kowalski's philosophy on UI polish, component design, animation decisions, and the invisible details that make software feel great.
skill.summary: This skill encodes Emil Kowalski's philosophy on UI polish, component design, animation decisions, and the invisible details that make software feel great.
skill.author: emilkowalski

security_audit.summary: All 153 static analyzer findings are false positives. The skill is a single markdown file (SKILL.md) containing design engineering documentation with CSS and JavaScript code examples. The backtick characters flagged as 'external_commands' are markdown code fence delimiters, not shell execution. URLs flagged as 'network' are documentation hyperlinks (animations.dev, easing.dev, easings.co), not runtime network requests. Blocker findings for 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' have no basis in this file which contains only UI design guidelines. No prompt injection or malicious content detected.

content.user_title: Build polished UI with expert design guidance
content.value_statement: Most interfaces lack the invisible details that make software feel right. This skill applies proven design engineering principles for animations, transitions, and component polish to help you build interfaces users love.
actual_capabilities: Review UI code and provide specific before/after improvements using the Design Engineering Review Format
Apply the Animation Decision Framework to determine if, how, and when elements should animate
Recommend appropriate easing curves, durations, and spring configurations for different UI patterns
Identify and fix common animation anti-patterns like transition: all, scale(0), and ease-in on UI elements
Guide component building with proven defaults from battle-tested libraries like Sonner and Radix UI
Advise on performance optimization using hardware-accelerated CSS transforms and transitions
limitations: This skill provides design guidance only and does not execute code or run commands
Code examples use CSS, JavaScript, and React patterns but require the developer to implement them in their project
Animation values and easing curves are recommendations that should be tested on target devices and browsers
Accessibility guidance covers prefers-reduced-motion but does not replace a full accessibility audit

use_cases.title: Polish production UI components
use_cases.description: A frontend developer reviews their modal, drawer, and tooltip components against the Animation Decision Framework to ensure proper easing, timing, and interruptibility.

use_cases.title: Establish design system animation standards
use_cases.description: A design engineer uses the Core Philosophy and CSS Transform Mastery sections to define consistent animation tokens for a component library.

use_cases.title: Fix animation performance issues
use_cases.description: A developer experiencing dropped frames during page loads applies the hardware acceleration and CSS vs JavaScript animation guidance to resolve the bottleneck.

prompt_templates.title: Review my UI component
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this button component and tell me what animation and transition improvements would make it feel more polished.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Getting design feedback on a single component

prompt_templates.title: Decide if this should animate
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a command palette that users open 100+ times per day. Should I add an open and close animation? Apply the Animation Decision Framework to decide.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Using the frequency-based decision guide

prompt_templates.title: Fix my animation performance
prompt_templates.prompt: My dashboard animations drop frames during page load. I am using Framer Motion with the x and y shorthand properties. Show me how to fix this for hardware acceleration.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Resolving a specific performance problem

prompt_templates.title: Build a custom toast notification system
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a toast component like Sonner. Guide me through the transition strategy, toast positioning with translateY, momentum-based dismissal, and edge case handling for tab visibility and stacked hover states.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Building a production-ready component from scratch

output: Change transition: all to transition: transform 200ms ease-out for GPU acceleration
Replace scale(0) with scale(0.95) and opacity: 0 for a more natural entrance
Switch ease-in to ease-out or a custom curve like cubic-bezier(0.23, 1, 0.32, 1)

output_examples.input: Should my sidebar navigation animate when expanding and collapsing?
output: Apply the Animation Decision Framework: users interact with sidebar navigation tens of times per day. Consider a subtle height transition or skip animation entirely for keyboard-initiated toggles.
If animating: use ease-out at 200ms max duration, animate only transform and opacity, and ensure the animation is interruptible with CSS transitions.

best_practices: Always specify exact CSS properties in transitions instead of using transition: all
Use CSS transitions over keyframes for any UI element that can be triggered rapidly
Test animations on real devices at full speed, slow motion, and frame by frame before shipping
anti_patterns: Using transition: all which triggers expensive layout and paint calculations
Animating from scale(0) which looks unnatural as nothing in the real world disappears and reappears completely
Using ease-in for UI animations which starts slow and makes the interface feel sluggish

faq.question: What is the Animation Decision Framework?
faq.answer: A four-step process to evaluate every animation: 1) Should it animate at all based on usage frequency, 2) What is the purpose, 3) What easing to use based on enter or exit, 4) How fast based on element type. This prevents unnecessary or poorly-timed animations.

faq.question: Why avoid transition: all in CSS?
faq.answer: Using transition: all triggers layout and paint steps for every property change. Specifying exact properties like transition: transform 200ms ease-out runs on the GPU and avoids expensive recalculations, keeping animations smooth.

faq.question: When should I use CSS animations versus JavaScript animations?
faq.answer: Use CSS animations for predetermined, static animations that run off the main thread. Use JavaScript animations like Framer Motion for dynamic, interruptible animations that respond to user input. CSS stays smooth under load while JavaScript can drop frames.

faq.question: What easing curve should I use for UI interactions?
faq.answer: Use ease-out for entering elements and ease-in-out for on-screen movement. Avoid the default CSS easings and use custom curves like cubic-bezier(0.23, 1, 0.32, 1) for a stronger, more intentional feel. Never use ease-in for UI animations.

faq.question: How do I make Framer Motion animations hardware-accelerated?
faq.answer: Avoid the shorthand x, y, and scale properties which run on the main thread. Instead, use the full transform string like animate={{ transform: 'translateX(100px)' }} which runs on the GPU and stays smooth during page loads.

faq.question: Should I disable all animations for users with motion sensitivity?
faq.answer: No. Reduced motion means fewer and gentler animations, not zero. Keep opacity and color transitions that aid comprehension. Remove movement and position animations using the prefers-reduced-motion media query.
